Esorb Inc. is a company that started in 1908 and has become an international successful company with about 26,000 employees at 62 locations in 23 countries.  It started as a trading company for automobile accessories and eventually expanded into the production of automobile components in Germany.  In the 1950s Esorb manufactured handles, hatches, hinges, latches, sun visors, defrostable windows and canisters.  They were the first company to produce power windows starting in the 1960s.  They eventually started producing seat recliners and adjustable back rests for automobiles.  By the 1970s they were the first to produce power seat adjusters and electronic control-units for power window regulators in the 1980s.  At the end of the nineties ESORB had production sites in Germany, England, Spain, America, Mexico, Japan and France.  By 2005 ESORB had 8900 employees in 37 locations and in 19 different countries.  Throughout the years ESORB continued to strive for lightweight design to optimize fuel efficiency and CO2 emissions.  In 2008 ESORB added electric drives for window regulators, sunroofs and seatbelt retractors to its product range.  In 2009 it formed joint ventures in China and Thailand to start production in those locations.  In 2011 ESORB entered a joint venture in South Korea.  In 2014 they built new facilities in South Africa.  Now in 2022 the core competence of Esorb Inc. is the production of technology for doors and liftgates, adjustment systems for front and rear seats and electric motors and drives.


Esorb Inc. strives to play active roles in shaping the social environment.  They use environmentally friendly technology and are involved in the communities they are in.  They are involved with food drives, habitat for humanity and mental health charities.  They are committed to protecting the health and safety of their employees.  The principles of ESORB Inc are: putting the company’s interest ahead of their own, to be innovative to secure leading market position and best price-performance ratio, acting fairly towards employees on all levels and in all locations, delivering top performance to their customers, all stakeholders act as one team with trust, making fast decisions and assuming responsibility for their actions.  

In 2005 ESORB Inc started two plants in London, Ontario, Canada.  There are currently over 400 employees who work in production, skilled trades, administration and management in London, Ontario.  In 2005 ESORB mainly produced seat adjusters, seat components and doors systems.  Now they focus on the production of seat systems.  ESORB in London, Ontario’s main customers are Volkswagon, Ford, and Chrylser.  The London, Ontario plant has received the London Chamber of Commerce Excellence in Human Resources Award, Ivey Academy Manufacturer of the Year, ASQ Quality Award of Excellence, London Economic Leadership Award, the FCA supplier of the Year Award, Volkswagon “A: Rating and Ford Q1 Certification. 

As ESORB is a global company, they manage the procurement of production materials, services and capital equipment through a global sourcing network.  They impose challenging standards on suppliers.  In return they offer stable relationship with growth prospects with their suppliers.  

Christine Jackson, a buyer at ESORB in London, Ontario, has sent out requests for quotations for a wiring harness they use in their doors for a new automobile model.  Marketing estimates that the demand for harness is 5,000 units a month in year one with a 20% growth expected for year two.  The chosen supplier will incur some costs to retool for this particular harness.  The harness will be prepackaged in 24 x 12 x 6 inch cartons.  Each packaged unit weighs approximately 10 pounds.   

As with most automotive component companies, adequate supplier capacity is a critical issue.  Taking a lesson from the demands placed on it by its current customers, Esorb Inc. will seek some assurance from its suppliers that they can increase the supply of components by 25% within four week’s notice of changing market conditions.  Supplier responsiveness and ability to satisfy Esorb Inc.’s volume requirements will be critical.

Wire Harness Market Information

Esorb Inc. recognizes that demand is growing rapidly, although actual numbers are difficult to obtain.  One of the challenges ESORB faces, and perhaps a major reason why ESORB may want to quickly “lock in” supplier capacity, is that other products use similar wire harnesses as are used in the doors and liftgates and cars are having more and more components that are powered and require wire harnesses.  

In sum, ESORB Inc must share their market with the growing power and automotive market, and assess their relative leverage in the market given other market demands for the wire harnesses.


ESORB Inc is targeting the price of this particular wire harness at $30.  This is not unreasonable given current market pricing.  However, the market for wire harnesses extends well beyond the automotive industry, and is not without its share of uncertainty and disruptions.

In the automotive industry, today's customers demand defect-free products.  With intense price competition and narrow profit margins, a single product defect, particularly when the automobile is in the customers' hands, can "wipe out" any profit from the sale.  Poor quality will also adversely affect market reputation and future sales.  Although exact numbers are difficult to obtain, financial analysts at Esorb Inc. calculate, based on experience and assumptions, that each defect will result, on average, in $130 in non-conformance costs that Esorb Inc. must bear (including lost customer goodwill).

The company plans to introduce the new line of door technology to the market place in March 2023.  It must have inventory by January 2019 to begin process proving and pilot production.  The March date coincides with preparing for the new model year sales season.  It is now early August 2022.

Esorb Inc. relies on cross-functional commodity teams to develop sourcing strategies for key purchased items.  This team is led by buyer Christine Jackson.  Executive management views the supplier selection decision as a critical part of the new door technology development.  The commodity team has spent the last several weeks visiting four wire harness suppliers and is currently evaluating various supply options. The team expects to begin negotiation with one or more suppliers within the next several weeks. Information regarding the suppliers under consideration is presented in the following section.

THE SUPPLY ALTERNATIVES


The team developed a market analysis of wire harness suppliers, and narrowed their search to four specific suppliers.   Requests for quotations for the wiring harness were sent to the four prospective suppliers.  These four suppliers were selected as final contenders based on: a) cost competitiveness given ESORBs initial target cost, and/or b) location proximity to ESORB’s London, Ontario assembly site.  The team was somewhat divided, as some members felt that ESORB should globalize its sourcing initiatives, while others felt that local suppliers would be a better choice in terms of working arrangements. Engineering supported the commodity team's preliminary efforts by purchasing off-the-shelf wire harness’s for testing.  This helped determine if the suppliers had a product that initially satisfied ESORB’s expectations.  Relying on product samples, while providing preliminary insight into the capability and technology of each supplier, was not sufficient to support a final supplier selection decision.  Hence, the need for direct visits by the commodity team became apparent.

The team decided to visit the four suppliers directly to collect detailed information.  The visits ranged from one to two days each, with both visits completed within a two-week period.  These visits were time-consuming and exhausting, particularly since two suppliers are in China.   Esorb Inc. does not have an International Purchasing Office (IPO) to support its international procurement activities.  Furthermore, no one on the team spoke Chinese. Fortunately, the other suppliers are located in the Ontario and were much easier to visit.  In fact they were located only 50 kilometers from the ESORB’s assembly facility.  The following sections summarize data collected during the commodity team's visits to the two suppliers.

All Wired Up

The first quote received was received from All Wired Up in Woodstock, Ontario.  Woodstock is about 50 kilometers from ESORB’s facility so the quote was delivered in person.  When Christine and the buying team toured their facility they were greeted by the sales agent and an engineering representative. While touring the facility the sales agent noted that engineering would be happy to work closely with ESORB in developing the unit and would also be interested in future business that might involve finding ways to reduce costs.  The sales agent also noted that they were hungry for business, as they were losing a lot of customers to companies from China.  The quote included unit price, tooling and packaging.  The quoted unit price does not include shipping costs.  All Wired Up requires no special warehousing of inventory, and daily deliveries from its manufacturing site directly to ESORB’s assembly operation are possible.

During the team's visit the plant manager claimed that capacity was not an issue, and that the company would be willing to commit the required production capacity to the ESORB contract.  Similar wiring harnesses accounted for about 40% of All Wired Up's $60 million in 2021 sales.  The plant is currently at 90% capacity.

The plant manager pointed out that the company has committed significant resources to setting up a JIT production system for the wiring harness line. Indeed, the ESORB team was impressed with the performance of the kanban signals and flow-through workstations.  All Wired Up also had a solid reputation within the industry for working with its customers on future product development. 

Upon visiting the quality department, the quality manager seemed particularly preoccupied and "on edge."  When the plant manager left for a few minutes to answer a phone call, the group asked the quality manager if the company had experienced any significant problems recently.  He confessed that the last shipment of Wiring Harnesses had several quality problems, and the number of returns from large distributors had increased dramatically.  This was creating some fairly severe disruptions to production scheduling and delivery.  However, he assured the ESORB team that the design engineers were working full-time on the problem and that it would be solved well before ESORB placed an order.  When the plant manager returned, the quality manager made no further mention of the problem.  The plant manager estimated that the ramp-up time for the first shipment would be very short, approximately 3 months.  


Unit price = $30 CAD
Packaging costs = $0.75 per unit
Tooling = $6,000 one-time fixed charge
Freight cost = $5.20 per hundred pounds
On-time delivery record = 99.0%
Quality = 4,000 PPM defects


Good Luck Assemblies

The second quote received is from Good Luck Assemblies (GLA) of Guangdong Province, China.  The supplier must pack the harnesses in a container and ship via inland transportation to the port of Shanghai in China, have the shipment transferred to a container ship, ship material to Halifax Nova Scotia, and then have material transported inland to London.  The quoted unit price does not include international shipping costs, which the buyer will assume. 

GLA was the largest supplier the team visited (sales of $1 billion).  The plant covered ten acres, with a wide variety of automotive components produced in the facility.  Wiring harnesses represent a large segment of GLA production (GLA commits 15% of total capacity to wiring harnesses and derives 15 % of revenues from the sales of these power systems).  Because of its size, however, the company seemed most interested in large contracts ($1 million or more annually). The plant is currently at 97% capacity.   

The highest-ranking manager that met with the ESORB team was a sales manager, who took the team to visit various departments.  The division vice-president and plant manager were in conference with a wiring harness customer, who the ESORB team found out had formed a strategic supply alliance with GLA.  The ESORB commodity team felt a bit "snubbed" at the facility, particularly the group's female members.  The facility was efficient, spotless, and modern. 

When the team visited engineering, they spoke with a manager in design.  The engineer estimated, based on previous experience, that the ramp-up time to begin production that would satisfy Esorb’s specifications would be about 2 months

The sales manager was particularly proud of GLA’s new Internet-based electronic data ERP system.  This system allowed direct communication with customers.  He was also proud that GLA was "the price leader" for the industry, and was producing Wiring Harnesses for several of the major automotive companies.  He also talked about the company's extensive investment in research and development.  When the sales manager heard that the Wiring Harness order, based on 60,000 units in year one, would likely not exceed $2 million per year, he hesitated, saying that he would need to discuss the order with management.  Moreover, he indicated that the company typically was not interested in orders of less than $10 million per annum, but that exceptions might be possible.  The economics associated with large orders is what made GLA a low-cost producer.

GLA Quote:

Unit Price = $19.50 CAD (quoted at $1 CAD = 5.09 Chinese Yen)
Shipping lead time = Eight weeks
Tooling = $3,000 CAD
On-time delivery record = 95% on-time (for large customers)
Quality = 9,500 PPM defects
Ramp-up time = 2 months


In additional to the supplier’s quote, Christine must consider additional costs and information before preparing a comparison of the Chinese supplier’s quotation:
•	Each monthly shipment requires three 40-foot containers.
•	Packing costs for the containerization = $2 per unit.
•	Cost of inland transportation to port export = $200 per container.
•	Freight forwarder’s fee = $100 per shipment (letter of credit, documentation, etc.)
•	Cost of ocean transport = $4,000 per container.  This has risen significantly in recent years due to a shortage of ocean freight capacity.
•	Marine insurance = $0.50 per $100 of shipment
•	CAD port handling charges = $1,200 per container.  This fee has also rise considerably this year, due to increased security.  Ports have also been complaining that the charges may increase in the future.
•	Customs duty = 5% of unit cost.
•	Customs broker fees per shipment = $300
•	Transportation from Halifax to London = $18.60 per hundred pounds.
•	Need to warehouse at least four weeks of inventory in London at a warehousing cost of $1.00 per cubic foot per month, to compensate for lead time uncertainty
•	Costs associated with committing corporate capital for holding inventory 15% per year
•	Cost of hedging currency – broker fees = $400 shipment
•	Estimated additional administrative time due to international shipping = 4 hours per shipment x $25 per hour
•	At least two five-day visits per year to travel to China to meet with supplier and provide updates on performance and shipping = $20,000 per year (estimated)


Amissiontech Co.

Amissiontech Co, is also a Chinese company located in Guangdon, China.  Amissiontech provided a bid of $21.50 CAD per unit.  During the team's visit the plant manager claimed that capacity was not an issue, and that the company would be willing to commit the required production capacity to the Wiring Harness contract.  Similar Wiring Harnesses accounted for about 40% of Amissiontech's $30 million in 2021 sales.  The plant was currently at 90% capacity.


The commodity team felt much more comfortable at Amissiontech than at the other Chinese supplier’s.  While this supplier has minimal experience doing business with North American firms, the company seemed quite anxious for the contract.  The company has several large Taiwanese and Japanese automotive manufacturers as customers.  At this time Amissiontech has no Canadian facilities or support staff.  The team had some concerns about becoming Amissiontech’s first major North American customer.

The company's product was excellent.  Every Wiring Harness system went through an extensive testing procedure that assured few problems would occur.  In fact, Amissiontech’s process control and testing were more thorough than any other supplier the team visited.  However, the combination of the testing process and geographic distance meant that delivery cycle times were much longer, up to 9 weeks per order, although the on-time delivery performance for the facility was excellent.  The team was not sure if current Asian delivery performance would be indicative of delivery performance to the Canada.  The facility appeared well maintained, clean, and orderly.  The team noticed that the wiring harness facility was extremely busy and wondered if the plant manager's claim about adequate capacity was accurate.  All employees worked closely together in work cells and knew each other by name.  Industry experts viewed Amissiontech as one of the most promising and dynamic companies in the industry.  The ramp-up time for the delivery of the first shipment was quoted as 3 months.  Relevant Amissiontech data include:

Quoted price = $21.50 (quoted at $1 CAD = 5.09 Chinese Yen)
Shipping Lead Time = 9 weeks
On-time delivery record = 99.0%
Quality = 4,000 PPM defects
Tooling = $2,500 CAD
Ramp Up time = 3 months

In additional to the supplier’s quote, Christine must consider additional costs and information before preparing a comparison of the Chinese supplier’s quotation:
•	Each monthly shipment requires three 40-foot containers.
•	Packing costs for the containerization = $2 per unit.
•	Cost of inland transportation to port export = $200 per container.
•	Freight forwarder’s fee = $100 per shipment (letter of credit, documentation, etc.)
•	Cost of ocean transport = $4,000 per container.  This has risen significantly in recent years due to a shortage of ocean freight capacity.
•	Marine insurance = $0.50 per $100 of shipment
•	CAD port handling charges = $1,200 per container.  This fee has also rise considerably this year, due to increased security.  Ports have also been complaining that the charges may increase in the future.
•	Customs duty = 5% of unit cost.
•	Customs broker fees per shipment = $300
•	Transportation from Halifax to London = $18.60 per hundred pounds.
•	Need to warehouse at least four weeks of inventory in London at a warehousing cost of $1.00 per cubic foot per month, to compensate for lead time uncertainty
•	Costs associated with committing corporate capital for holding inventory 15% per year
•	Cost of hedging currency – broker fees = $400 shipment
•	Estimated additional administrative time due to international shipping = 4 hours per shipment x $25 per hour
•	At least two five-day visits per year to travel to China to meet with supplier and provide updates on performance and shipping = $20,000 per year (estimated)

TCP Cable

A fourth candidate for the contract is TCP Cable, a manufacturer located in Cambridge, Ontario.  Over the past 5 years, TCP Cable has earned the reputation of providing its clients the highest level of service coupled with the highest quality of workmanship.  TCP derives 50% of its $25 million in revenues from automotive wire harnesses. The team discovered this company almost by accident.  A team member was browsing a trade journal and saw TCP’s advertisement.  When the team visited the facility, the team was surprised at its small size and by the fact that it is located in an old warehouse.  TCP’s president met with the team in person.  He explained that he was a graduate of Waterloo University in electrical engineering and had decided to start his own company after working for 15 years.  The company entered the wire harness market four years ago and has been producing a similar wire harness for just over a year.  During this time, however, TCP has established a reputation for delivery reliability and innovation.  The president explained that TCP’s success was based largely on its commitment to develop new technology, especially technology that enhanced product reliability.  He also claimed that he knew every customer personally.  Electronics Weekly had praised the company’s products in several recent editions.  However, the company was definitely a small growing entity (with less than 4% of global market share), but they expressed their intent to focus more on the Wire Harnesses.  The plant was currently at 88% capacity.

Everyone in the plant seemed highly motivated, and, except for the president, the team did not see any person who appeared over the age of 35.  The president was particularly excited about the possibility of working with ESORB, and promised to work with them closely on this contract and for any new product lines.  In particular, he emphasized that automotive electronics technology was improving and that the automation market was another sector that would continue to expand in the future.  Ramp-up time for the wire harness would be approximately 3.5 months.

TCP Cable requires no special warehousing of inventory, and daily deliveries from its manufacturing site directly to ESORB’s assembly operation are possible.


When asked if his firm would have any problem in meeting demand should they receive the contract, he hesitated before answering.  He admitted that this contract would be the largest in TCP’s relatively short history.  He also indicated that several other buying teams were also going to be sending teams to evaluate TCP within the next week.  However, he assured the team that he would do whatever it took to maintain reliable delivery schedules if TCP received the contract.  Interestingly, it appeared that the production lines were experiencing some problems during the team's visit, as they were shut down for nearly four hours!  Relevant TCP data include:

Unit price = $28 CAD
Packaging costs = $0.80 per unit
Tooling = $5,000 one-time fixed charge
Freight cost = $5.00 per hundred pounds
On-time delivery record = 95.0%
Quality = 4,500 PPM defects
Read and provide Immediate Issue and Other Issues of the above case study

Immediate Issue:
The primary immediate issue faced by Christine Jackson and the Esorb Inc. commodity team is to select the most appropriate supplier for the wiring harnesses needed in their new door technology. The decision needs to consider not only the quoted unit prices but also the total cost of ownership, including shipping costs, tooling charges, potential tariffs and customs fees, currency hedging, and warehousing costs. The team also needs to assess the reliability and quality of each supplier, their ability to meet ramp-up time and capacity requirements, and the anticipated growth in demand.

Quality deficiencies must be minimized, given their significant impact on non-conformance costs and the potential to damage Esorb Inc.'s reputation. The urgency of this issue is highlighted by the planned introduction of the new door technology in March 2023, with inventory needed by January 2019 for process proving and pilot production. Moreover, the negotiation with suppliers is expected to begin within a matter of weeks.

Other Issues:
1. **Global vs. Local Sourcing**: The commodity team is divided on whether to globalize sourcing, gaining possibly lower costs but also adding complexity, or to rely on local suppliers for better working arrangements and responsiveness.

2. **Supplier Capacity Commitment**: The supplier must be able to increase supply by 25% with short notice. This requirement must be balanced against current capacity utilization rates reported by suppliers.

3. **Long-Term Supplier Relationships**: Esorb Inc. values stable, long-term relationships with suppliers who can grow along with them. They need to assess which suppliers can offer this type of partnership.

4. **Quality Control Concerns**: Recent quality problems at All Wired Up and relatively high PPM defect rates among some of the suppliers need to be seriously considered as they will directly impact the brand image and customer satisfaction.

5. **Supply Chain Robustness**: The ability to establish a robust and reliable supply chain is critical, especially for international suppliers where lead times are long and disruptions can be costly.

6. **Technology and Innovation Alignment**: Esorb Inc.’s commitment to lightweight design and innovation requires suppliers who can support ongoing product development efforts.

7. **Environmental and Social Considerations**: As Esorb Inc. is involved in community and environmental initiatives, they likely prefer partners who also meet certain environmental and social standards.

8. **International Procurement Complexity**: Without an International Purchasing Office (IPO), the team faces challenges in language barriers, understanding foreign business customs, and handling international logistics complexities.

9. **Currency Risk and Hedging Costs**: With international suppliers, currency risk management becomes an additional cost of doing business that Christine will have to account for.

10. **Capacity Utilization and Expansion**: Suppliers are at high levels of capacity utilization; hence their ability to sustain quality while expanding operations to meet Esorb's needs needs to be validated.

In addressing these issues, Christine and the team have to perform a comprehensive supplier assessment, considering initial cost quotations as well as hidden costs and risks associated with each supplier. They must also ensure the chosen supplier aligns with Esorb Inc.'s company principles, including innovation, fair treatment of employees, high-performance delivery, and teamwork, which were listed as paramount to the company’s operations.
do a PEST analysis for the above case

A PEST analysis examines the Political, Economic, Social, and Technological factors that can affect an organization externally. Let's apply this framework to Esorb Inc., understanding the context of its global supply chain and the selection of a wiring harness supplier for its new door technology:

**Political Factors:**
1. **Tariffs and Trade Policies**: With suppliers in China and potential suppliers in Canada, changes in international trade policies, such as tariffs or trade agreements, could significantly affect costs and the ease of doing business across borders.
2. **Regulatory Environment**: Differences in regulations concerning product standards, environmental protection, and labor laws between China and Canada may impact supplier decisions.
3. **Customs and Import Regulations**: Customs procedures can influence lead times and costs. Political stability between countries of origin and destination can also affect the reliability and predictability of the supply chain.
4. **Local Economic Support**: Government incentives or subsidies for local businesses in Ontario might make local suppliers like All Wired Up or TCP Cable more competitive and favorable.

**Economic Factors:**
1. **Currency Exchange Rates**: Fluctuations in the CAD/Yen exchange rate can affect the cost-effectiveness of Chinese suppliers and might necessitate currency hedging strategies, adding complexity and cost.
2. **Global Economic Trends**: Economic downturns, or booms, can impact the purchasing power of consumers and thus the demand for automobiles, influencing the sales forecasts and volume requirements for parts like wiring harnesses.
3. **Transportation Costs**: The cost of shipping, influenced by fuel prices, availability of shipping containers, and freight capacity, is a significant consideration for international suppliers.
4. **Total Cost of Ownership**: Beyond the initial price quotes, the total cost to Esorb, including tooling, packaging, transportation, tariffs, and potential unexpected costs like quality issues, needs to be accurately evaluated.

**Social Factors:**
1. **Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR)**: Esorb Inc. is involved in various community and environmental initiatives. Thus, the social practices of potential suppliers, like environmental friendliness and fair labor conditions, could influence the selection process.
2. **Cultural Differences**: Challenges in communication, negotiation, and relationship management with suppliers from different cultural backgrounds (especially in China) could be a factor.
3. **Consumer Preferences**: Trends in consumer preferences for vehicles and the associated demand for features such as power windows and seats could continue to drive the need for wiring harnesses, affecting demand and supply strategies.

**Technological Factors:**
1. **Product Innovation**: Suppliers' capabilities to innovate, particularly in creating lighter and more environmentally friendly components, are crucial in aligning with Esorb Inc.'s principles and market position.
2. **Quality Control Technology**: The utilization of advanced quality control processes, testing procedures, and defect tracking could be determinants in choosing a supplier to ensure high-quality products.
3. **Supply Chain Management Systems**: The sophistication of suppliers' supply chain management systems, including their ERP and JIT capabilities, can be a deciding factor in ensuring efficient operations and communication.

Conducting this PEST analysis allows Esorb Inc. to understand the broader context within which its suppliers operate and the external factors influencing its supply chain. This helps in making a more informed and strategic supplier selection decision, taking into account not only the immediate costs and benefits but also the long-term implications of each potential supplier relationship.
Evaluate the single sourcing option versus multiple sourcing and provide 2 advantages and disadvantages for each

Single Sourcing Option:
Single sourcing is the practice of sourcing a particular component or service from only one supplier.

**Advantages:**
1. **Stronger Supplier Relationships**: Working closely with a single supplier can lead to a stronger, more collaborative relationship. This might result in better terms, priority treatment for urgent orders, and the supplier's willingness to invest in dedicated production capacity or technology tailored to the company's needs.
2. **Simplified Logistics and Management**: Logistics, communication, and quality control are simpler with single sourcing. There are fewer points of contact, better consistency in component quality, and potentially more straightforward inventory and shipment tracking.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Risk of Supply Disruption**: Depending heavily on one supplier makes a company vulnerable to supply disruptions due to various reasons such as financial issues, natural disasters, political unrest, or production failures at the supplier's end.
2. **Potentially Higher Costs**: The lack of competition may lead to higher prices or complacency in cost management. Single suppliers who are aware of their exclusive status might be less motivated to offer competitive pricing or cost reductions over time.

Multiple Sourcing Option:
Multiple sourcing involves procuring a particular component or service from several suppliers.

**Advantages:**
1. **Reduced Supply Risks**: By diversifying the supply base, a company is less vulnerable to disruptions. If one supplier faces problems, the company can quickly pivot to others to maintain the production schedule and meet customer demand.
2. **Cost Competitiveness**: Multiple suppliers stimulate competition, which can lead to better pricing, service, and innovation as suppliers vie for a greater share of the business. It can keep costs down and push suppliers to continually improve.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Complexity in Management**: Managing multiple suppliers can significantly increase the complexity of procurement, including the need for more contracts, negotiations, quality control processes, and coordination of logistics.
2. **Diluted Relationships and Purchasing Power**: Relationships with individual suppliers might not be as strong, given the divided attention and business volume. This could lead to less leverage in negotiations and weaker collaborative ties for product development or problem-solving.

When choosing between single and multiple sourcing strategies, a business must consider the nature of the product, the market's supply dynamics, the criticality of the component to production, and the company's appetite for risk. Both strategies have their trade-offs, and the decision will often come down to balancing the benefits of close supplier relationships and reduced complexity against the benefits of competition and risk mitigation.
Evaluate the short versus long-term contracts  and provide 2 advantages and disadvantages for each

Short-term Contracts:
Short-term contracts typically span a limited period, often one year or less, and are used for immediate needs or when future demand is uncertain.

**Advantages:**
1. **Flexibility**: Short-term contracts allow companies to respond quickly to market changes, adjust to new suppliers, or renegotiate terms more frequently to take advantage of cost benefits or new technologies.
2. **Reduced Commitment**: With a short-term contract, the buyer is not locked into a long-term arrangement, making it easier to address performance issues, switch suppliers if necessary, or capitalize on a falling market price for materials.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Higher Costs**: Due to the lack of long-term commitment, suppliers might charge higher prices to offset the increased risk and lack of guaranteed future business.
2. **Supply Uncertainty**: Short-term contracts might lead to less priority from the supplier's side, as they may prioritize clients with longer contracts. This may sometimes result in supply instability and lack of dedicated capacity for the buyer.

Long-term Contracts:
Long-term contracts span multiple years and indicate a commitment to a sustained supplier relationship, providing stability for both parties.

**Advantages:**
1. **Cost Savings**: By committing to a supplier for a longer period, buyers can often negotiate better prices due to the guaranteed business and larger volumes, leading to economies of scale.
2. **Stable Supply Chain**: Long-term contracts provide stability and predictability for both the supplier and the buyer, which facilitates better capacity planning, inventory management, and investment in dedicated technology or processes.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Reduced Flexibility**: Long-term engagements can restrict the buyer's ability to respond to market changes, new suppliers, or technological advancements, possibly resulting in missed opportunities or overpayment if market prices decline.
2. **Potential for Lock-In with Underperforming Supplier**: If the chosen supplier's performance declines or if they fail to keep up with technological advancements, the buyer may be contractually stuck with an underperforming partner, potentially affecting product quality and competitiveness.

In choosing the appropriate contract length, companies need to assess their requirements for agility, cost, supplier performance, and market volatility. It's also not uncommon for businesses to use a combination of both short and long-term agreements or opt for flexible long-term contracts with escape clauses to strike a balance between the two approaches.
provide a list of resources relating to beer micro breweries and the Enterprise resource planning system they use for managing invenory

Microbreweries often require specialized Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) systems that can handle the unique aspects of the brewing process, inventory management, distribution, and sales. Here is a list of resources and ERP systems that are either designed for or compatible with the operations of microbreweries:

**ERP Systems for Microbreweries:**

1. **OrchestratedBeer** - An all-in-one brewery management software solution built on SAP Business One. It handles everything from production to sales, inventory, and finance.
   Website: [OrchestratedBeer](https://www.orchestratedbeer.com/)

2. **Ekos Brewmaster** - An ERP system tailored for craft breweries. It helps manage inventory, production, sales, and accounting in one platform.
   Website: [Ekos Brewmaster](https://www.goekos.com/)

3. **VicinityBrew** - An ERP system with flexible brewing and packaging functionality and scalability for microbreweries and large-scale operations alike.
   Website: [VicinityBrew](https://www.vicinitybrew.com/)

4. **BrewPlanner** - A production planning and inventory management software designed specifically for craft brewers.
   Website: [BrewPlanner](https://www.brewplanner.com/)

5. **OBeer Brewery Software** - Offers inventory management, production, scheduling, and more, all designed for breweries.
   Website: [OBeer](https://www.obeer.com/)

6. **Unleashed Software** - An inventory management system that can be tailored to suit the needs of a craft brewery.
   Website: [Unleashed Software](https://www.unleashedsoftware.com/)

7. **Katana** - A manufacturing ERP that offers real-time inventory tracking and can be used by microbreweries for inventory and production management.
   Website: [Katana MRP](https://katanamrp.com/)

8. **Beer30** - Provides tools for managing every part of brewery operations, from inventory to production to keg tracking.

Established in 2014, the Toboggan Brewing Company is a microbrewery, located on
Richmond Street in London, Ontario. The Microbrewery is located onsite of the brew
pub, restaurant and retail outlet. The brewery focuses on quality small batch production
with a selection of year-round flagship beers and select seasonal offerings.
The founders and operators of the company have built the brewery and brand over the
past ten years to the point that they are considering a more comprehensive Enterprise
Resource Planning (ERP) system to manage inventory inputs, outputs and future
revenue growth.
Toboggan is interested in a comprehensive review of microbrewery inventory
management best practices, benchmarks, key performance indicators (KPIs) and the
types of ERP systems available for the microbrewery industry.
The cost of the ERP system is a consideration given current revenue and cost structure.
A KPI dashboard (either as part of the ERP system or as an external platform such as
Tableau, Microsoft Power BI or alternative) is also of interest.

For Toboggan Brewing Company, selecting the right ERP system will be pivotal in efficiently managing inventory, streamlining operations, and supporting future growth. Here's a structured approach for conducting a comprehensive review:

**Inventory Management Best Practices for Microbreweries:**

- **Centralized Data Management**: Implement a single source of truth for inventory levels, production schedules, and sales information to reduce errors and increase transparency.
- **Real-Time Inventory Tracking**: Implement RFID or barcode scanning for real-time inventory updates.
- **Demand Forecasting**: Use historical sales data and predictive analytics to accurately forecast demand, optimize stock levels, and prevent overproduction or stockouts.
- **Supplier Relationship Management**: Develop close relationships with suppliers to ensure a reliable supply chain and perhaps secure better pricing or payment terms.
- **Batch Tracking and Quality Control**: Track each batch of product from ingredients through production to delivery for quality assurance and easier recall management if necessary.
- **Regular Auditing**: Perform regular inventory audits to ensure that the ERP system matches what's physically in stock.

**Benchmarks and Key Performance Indicators (KPIs):**

- **Inventory Turnover Rate**: Measures how many times inventory is sold and replaced over a period; a higher turnover indicates efficient inventory management.
- **Carrying Cost of Inventory**: Includes storage, insurance, and other costs related to holding inventory; should be minimized.
- **Stockout and Overstock Rate**: Monitors the frequency of stockouts and overstocks, which can impact customer satisfaction and holding costs.
- **Order Accuracy Rate**: Assesses the correctness of order fulfillments.
- **Yield Per Batch**: Measures the efficiency of the production process in terms of output quality and quantity.

**Types of ERP Systems for Microbreweries:**

- **OrchestratedBeer**: Built on SAP Business One, this cloud-based ERP solution offers brewery-specific capabilities including TTB reporting, inventory management, and financials.
- **Ekos Brewmaster**: An ERP tailored for craft breweries, with features for managing production, sales, inventory, and accounting.
- **VicinityBrew**: Provides scalability and flexibility, integrating easily with platforms like Microsoft Dynamics, to support production, inventory, and distribution.
- **Unleashed Software**: While not brewery-specific, it provides robust inventory management and can integrate with other business platforms.
  
**Cost Considerations:**

- **Subscription vs. Ownership**: Consider whether a cloud-based subscription model or a one-time purchase with ongoing support costs makes more financial sense.
- **Customization and Integration Costs**: Analyze costs associated with customizing the ERP to fit specific business operations and integrating with existing systems.
- **Training and Implementation**: Consider costs for training staff and the potential productivity loss during the implementation phase.

**KPI Dashboards:**

- **Internal ERP Dashboards**: Some ERP systems include built-in analytics and dashboard reporting.
- **Tableau**: A powerful data visualization tool that can create comprehensive dashboards incorporating data from various sources.
- **Microsoft Power BI**: Known for its user-friendly interface and integration with Microsoft products, good for compiling KPIs for analysis.
- **QlikView**: Offers guided analytics and is suited for companies that require custom-built data applications and dashboards.

**Next Steps for Toboggan Brewing Company:**

1. **Requirements Gathering**: Identify specific needs and processes within the microbrewery that the ERP system must address.
2. **Vendor Evaluation**: Schedule demos with ERP vendors and evaluate the systems against identified requirements and cost considerations.
3. **System Demo and Testing**: Engage with short-listed vendors for in-depth demonstrations and, if possible, a trial period to test compatibility.
4. **Request for Proposals (RFPs)**: Issue RFPs outlining the brewery's requirements and use the responses to assess functionality, support, and costs.
5. **Integration Planning**: Plan for how the chosen ERP system will integrate with existing systems and processes.
6. **Training Strategy**: Develop a plan to train staff on the new system to ensure a smooth transition.
7. **Implementation and Scale**: Roll out the ERP system incrementally, starting with core functions and scaling up as proficiency across the organization increases.
8. **Performance Monitoring**: Establish procedures for regularly reviewing KPIs and system performance to ensure the ERP continues to meet the brewery's needs.

By following this structured approach, Toboggan Brewing Company can select an appropriate ERP system that will help manage its current operations efficiently and support future growth successfully.

**Company Overview and Current Processes:**

Esorb Inc. is a company that started in 1908 and has become an international successful company with about 26,000 employees at 62 locations in 23 countries. It started as a trading company for automobile accessories and eventually expanded into the production of automobile components in Germany. In the 1950s Esorb manufactured handles, hatches, hinges, latches, sun visors, defrostable windows and canisters. They were the first company to produce power windows starting in the 1960s. They eventually started producing seat recliners and adjustable back rests for automobiles. By the 1970s they were the first to produce power seat adjusters and electronic control-units for power window regulators in the 1980s. At the end of the nineties ESORB had production sites in Germany, England, Spain, America, Mexico, Japan and France. By 2005 ESORB had 8900 employees in 37 locations and in 19 different countries. Throughout the years ESORB continued to strive for lightweight design to optimize fuel efficiency and CO2 emissions. In 2008 ESORB added electric drives for window regulators, sunroofs and seatbelt retractors to its product range. In 2009 it formed joint ventures in China and Thailand to start production in those locations. In 2011 ESORB entered a joint venture in South Korea. In 2014 they built new facilities in South Africa. Now in 2022 the core competence of Esorb Inc. is the production of technology for doors and liftgates, adjustment systems for front and rear seats and electric motors and drives.


Esorb Inc. strives to play active roles in shaping the social environment. They use environmentally friendly technology and are involved in the communities they are in. They are involved with food drives, habitat for humanity and mental health charities. They are committed to protecting the health and safety of their employees. The principles of ESORB Inc are: putting the company’s interest ahead of their own, to be innovative to secure leading market position and best price-performance ratio, acting fairly towards employees on all levels and in all locations, delivering top performance to their customers, all stakeholders act as one team with trust, making fast decisions and assuming responsibility for their actions.

In 2005 ESORB Inc started two plants in London, Ontario, Canada. There are currently over 400 employees who work in production, skilled trades, administration and management in London, Ontario. In 2005 ESORB mainly produced seat adjusters, seat components and doors systems. Now they focus on the production of seat systems. ESORB in London, Ontario’s main customers are Volkswagon, Ford, and Chrylser. The London, Ontario plant has received the London Chamber of Commerce Excellence in Human Resources Award, Ivey Academy Manufacturer of the Year, ASQ Quality Award of Excellence, London Economic Leadership Award, the FCA supplier of the Year Award, Volkswagon “A: Rating and Ford Q1 Certification.

As ESORB is a global company, they manage the procurement of production materials, services and capital equipment through a global sourcing network. They impose challenging standards on suppliers. In return they offer stable relationship with growth prospects with their suppliers.

Christine Jackson, a buyer at ESORB in London, Ontario, has sent out requests for quotations for a wiring harness they use in their doors for a new automobile model. Marketing estimates that the demand for harness is 5,000 units a month in year one with a 20% growth expected for year two. The chosen supplier will incur some costs to retool for this particular harness. The harness will be prepackaged in 24 x 12 x 6 inch cartons. Each packaged unit weighs approximately 10 pounds.

As with most automotive component companies, adequate supplier capacity is a critical issue. Taking a lesson from the demands placed on it by its current customers, Esorb Inc. will seek some assurance from its suppliers that they can increase the supply of components by 25% within four week’s notice of changing market conditions. Supplier responsiveness and ability to satisfy Esorb Inc.’s volume requirements will be critical.

Wire Harness Market Information

Esorb Inc. recognizes that demand is growing rapidly, although actual numbers are difficult to obtain. One of the challenges ESORB faces, and perhaps a major reason why ESORB may want to quickly “lock in” supplier capacity, is that other products use similar wire harnesses as are used in the doors and liftgates and cars are having more and more components that are powered and require wire harnesses.

In sum, ESORB Inc must share their market with the growing power and automotive market, and assess their relative leverage in the market given other market demands for the wire harnesses.


ESORB Inc is targeting the price of this particular wire harness at $30. This is not unreasonable given current market pricing. However, the market for wire harnesses extends well beyond the automotive industry, and is not without its share of uncertainty and disruptions.

In the automotive industry, today’s customers demand defect-free products. With intense price competition and narrow profit margins, a single product defect, particularly when the automobile is in the customers’ hands, can “wipe out” any profit from the sale. Poor quality will also adversely affect market reputation and future sales. Although exact numbers are difficult to obtain, financial analysts at Esorb Inc. calculate, based on experience and assumptions, that each defect will result, on average, in $130 in non-conformance costs that Esorb Inc. must bear (including lost customer goodwill).

The company plans to introduce the new line of door technology to the market place in March 2023. It must have inventory by January 2019 to begin process proving and pilot production. The March date coincides with preparing for the new model year sales season. It is now early August 2022.

Esorb Inc. relies on cross-functional commodity teams to develop sourcing strategies for key purchased items. This team is led by buyer Christine Jackson. Executive management views the supplier selection decision as a critical part of the new door technology development. The commodity team has spent the last several weeks visiting four wire harness suppliers and is currently evaluating various supply options. The team expects to begin negotiation with one or more suppliers within the next several weeks. Information regarding the suppliers under consideration is presented in the following section.

THE SUPPLY ALTERNATIVES


The team developed a market analysis of wire harness suppliers, and narrowed their search to four specific suppliers. Requests for quotations for the wiring harness were sent to the four prospective suppliers. These four suppliers were selected as final contenders based on: a) cost competitiveness given ESORBs initial target cost, and/or b) location proximity to ESORB’s London, Ontario assembly site. The team was somewhat divided, as some members felt that ESORB should globalize its sourcing initiatives, while others felt that local suppliers would be a better choice in terms of working arrangements. Engineering supported the commodity team’s preliminary efforts by purchasing off-the-shelf wire harness’s for testing. This helped determine if the suppliers had a product that initially satisfied ESORB’s expectations. Relying on product samples, while providing preliminary insight into the capability and technology of each supplier, was not sufficient to support a final supplier selection decision. Hence, the need for direct visits by the commodity team became apparent.

The team decided to visit the four suppliers directly to collect detailed information. The visits ranged from one to two days each, with both visits completed within a two-week period. These visits were time-consuming and exhausting, particularly since two suppliers are in China. Esorb Inc. does not have an International Purchasing Office (IPO) to support its international procurement activities. Furthermore, no one on the team spoke Chinese. Fortunately, the other suppliers are located in the Ontario and were much easier to visit. In fact they were located only 50 kilometers from the ESORB’s assembly facility. The following sections summarize data collected during the commodity team’s visits to the two suppliers.

All Wired Up

The first quote received was received from All Wired Up in Woodstock, Ontario. Woodstock is about 50 kilometers from ESORB’s facility so the quote was delivered in person. When Christine and the buying team toured their facility they were greeted by the sales agent and an engineering representative. While touring the facility the sales agent noted that engineering would be happy to work closely with ESORB in developing the unit and would also be interested in future business that might involve finding ways to reduce costs. The sales agent also noted that they were hungry for business, as they were losing a lot of customers to companies from China. The quote included unit price, tooling and packaging. The quoted unit price does not include shipping costs. All Wired Up requires no special warehousing of inventory, and daily deliveries from its manufacturing site directly to ESORB’s assembly operation are possible.

During the team’s visit the plant manager claimed that capacity was not an issue, and that the company would be willing to commit the required production capacity to the ESORB contract. Similar wiring harnesses accounted for about 40% of All Wired Up’s $60 million in 2021 sales. The plant is currently at 90% capacity.

The plant manager pointed out that the company has committed significant resources to setting up a JIT production system for the wiring harness line. Indeed, the ESORB team was impressed with the performance of the kanban signals and flow-through workstations. All Wired Up also had a solid reputation within the industry for working with its customers on future product development.

Upon visiting the quality department, the quality manager seemed particularly preoccupied and “on edge.” When the plant manager left for a few minutes to answer a phone call, the group asked the quality manager if the company had experienced any significant problems recently. He confessed that the last shipment of Wiring Harnesses had several quality problems, and the number of returns from large distributors had increased dramatically. This was creating some fairly severe disruptions to production scheduling and delivery. However, he assured the ESORB team that the design engineers were working full-time on the problem and that it would be solved well before ESORB placed an order. When the plant manager returned, the quality manager made no further mention of the problem. The plant manager estimated that the ramp-up time for the first shipment would be very short, approximately 3 months.

Packaging costs = $0.75 per unit

The supplier must pack the harnesses in a container and ship via inland transportation to the port of Shanghai in China, have the shipment transferred to a container ship, ship material to Halifax Nova Scotia, and then have material transported inland to London.
